title: The Growing Square: Where 2x Comes From

md: There is a classical calculus textbook, *Calculus Made Easy* by Silvanus P. Thompson (1910), that opens with a line worth keeping near you the whole time you study calculus:
> *"What one fool can do, another can."*
Calculus has a reputation for being hard. It is not, really. It is *unfamiliar* ‚Äî and the unfamiliar part is mostly **two symbols** that get used without ever being explained:
$d$ ‚Äî a tiny prefix meaning **"a little bit of"**. So $dx$ is *a little bit of* $x$. Not a fraction. Not a function. Just: a small change in $x$.
$\int$ ‚Äî a stretched-out letter S, meaning **"the sum of"**. So $\int f(x)\,dx$ is **the sum of** $f(x)$ multiplied by *a little bit of* $x$, added up.
Both of these symbols are 300 years old and were chosen for exactly the meanings above. Once you read them as plain English, calculus is mostly arithmetic.

blocks.title: The whole lesson in one sentence
blocks.body: We are going to *grow a square* by a little bit and watch what new area appears. That observation alone ‚Äî no limits, no formulas ‚Äî will hand us the derivative of $x^2$.

md: ## 1. A square that grows
Picture a square with side $x$. Its area is $x^2$ ‚Äî no calculus needed.
Now let $x$ grow by a tiny amount $dx$. The square gets a little wider AND a little taller. **Three new pieces of area appear**:
(a) a thin strip of width $dx$ along the **right** side ‚Äî area $x \cdot dx$
(b) a thin strip of width $dx$ along the **top** side ‚Äî area $x \cdot dx$
(c) a tiny **corner square** of side $dx$ ‚Äî area $(dx)^2$
Drag the sliders below ‚Äî change $x$ and $dx$ and watch the three new pieces.
Add the three new pieces:
$\text{new area} = x^2 + \underbrace{x\,dx + x\,dx}_{\text{two strips}} + \underbrace{(dx)^2}_{\text{tiny corner}} = x^2 + 2x\,dx + (dx)^2$
So the **growth** in area (what was *added*) is $2x\,dx + (dx)^2$.
Now here is the famous trick.

blocks.title: Different degrees of smallness
blocks.body:
If $dx$ is small ‚Äî say $dx = 0.01$ ‚Äî then $(dx)^2 = 0.0001$. A hundred times smaller. If $dx$ is even smaller, $(dx)^2$ is **vanishingly** smaller. The corner is always smaller than the strips, and it gets smaller *faster* as $dx$ shrinks.

Thompson called this difference "different degrees of smallness": both $dx$ and $(dx)^2$ are small, but $(dx)^2$ is *much* smaller. In the limit where $dx \to 0$, the corner doesn't just shrink ‚Äî it disappears compared to the strips. **Tick the "drop the corner" box in the widget.** The corner is gone. What's left is the truth about how the square grew.

md: ## 2. The derivative falls out
After dropping the negligible corner, the area's growth is
$\text{growth} = 2x \, dx$
Divide both sides by $dx$:
$\dfrac{\text{growth}}{dx} = 2x$
The left-hand side has a name. "Growth in area per little bit of $x$" is exactly what mathematicians call $\dfrac{d(x^2)}{dx}$ ‚Äî read it as **"a little bit of $x^2$ per little bit of $x$"**. So:
$\boxed{\;\dfrac{d(x^2)}{dx} = 2x\;}$
No limits, no $h \to 0$, no algebra. We *grew a square* and *looked at it*. That is calculus.
## 3. Same trick gives the power rule
What if the side were $x^3$ ‚Äî a cube growing by $dx$?
Three terms involve different degrees of smallness:
$3x^2\,dx$ ‚Äî proportional to $dx$ ‚Äî the **important** part
$3x\,(dx)^2$ ‚Äî proportional to $(dx)^2$ ‚Äî vanishingly small
$(dx)^3$ ‚Äî even more vanishingly small
Drop everything with $(dx)^2$ or higher. What remains is $3x^2 \, dx$. Divide by $dx$:
$\dfrac{d(x^3)}{dx} = 3x^2$

blocks.title: See the pattern
blocks.body:
$\dfrac{d(x^2)}{dx} = 2 x^{1}$

$\dfrac{d(x^3)}{dx} = 3 x^{2}$

$\dfrac{d(x^4)}{dx} = 4 x^{3}$ (try expanding $(x+dx)^4$ if you don't believe it)

**Guess what comes next** before reading on.

md: ## 4. The Power Rule
The pattern is:
$\boxed{\;\dfrac{d(x^n)}{dx} = n \, x^{\,n-1}\;}$

**Bring the exponent down in front; reduce the exponent by 1.** That is the most-used formula in all of calculus, and we discovered it without memorizing anything ‚Äî just by watching squares and cubes grow.
It also works for $n$ that isn't a whole number. For example, $\sqrt{x} = x^{1/2}$:
And for negative powers ‚Äî $\dfrac{1}{x} = x^{-1}$:

blocks.title: Two more rules you can guess
blocks.body:
**Constants vanish.** $\dfrac{d}{dx}(7) = 0$ ‚Äî a constant doesn't change, so it has no growth. (A square whose side is 7 ‚Äî fine. But a square whose *area* is always 7, no matter how $x$ moves ‚Äî it isn't growing at all.)

**Multiplied constants pass through.** $\dfrac{d}{dx}(7 x^2) = 7 \cdot 2x = 14x$. Multiplying $x^2$ by 7 just makes it grow 7 times faster; the *rule* for how it grows ($2x$) is unchanged.

md: ## 5. Why this matters

Almost every derivative in this course will reduce ‚Äî sooner or later ‚Äî to the power rule. Trig, log, exp derivatives are different formulas, but for **polynomials**, you now have a complete shortcut:
Bring the exponent down, reduce by 1, term by term. Constants vanish.
And the *reason* it works isn't a memorized formula. It is: **little bits of area get smaller faster than little bits of length, so corners drop out**. That's it.

blocks.title: What about limits?
blocks.body: Modern textbooks introduce the derivative as a *limit*: $f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \dfrac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$. That definition is rigorous and important ‚Äî and you should know it. But it tends to come BEFORE any intuition for what a derivative *is*. Thompson's growing-square argument is what you should picture in your head every time you see that limit. The limit is the formal version of *"drop the $(dx)^2$ corner"*.
